title: Einstein, P-adic Sudoku, Domino, and Decidability
speaker: Rachel Greenfeld
abstract: Translational tiling is a covering of a space (such as Euclidean space) using translated copies of one building block, called a "translational tile'', without any positive measure overlaps.

proof_stages.label: Aperiodic tiling question and translational specialization
proof_stages.summary: The talk moves from the classical Einstein problem to translation-only tilings, defines translational tiling and the tiling space, formulates the periodic tiling conjecture, and explains Wang's implication from periodicity to decidability.

proof_stages.label: Reduction landscape and counterexample target
proof_stages.summary: Known positive cases and transfer reductions are reviewed, then the main aperiodic target is stated: a counterexample in a virtually Z^2 group Z^2 x G_0, which can be pulled back to Z^d and inflated to R^d in sufficiently high dimension.

proof_stages.label: Aperiodic p-adic Sudoku puzzle
proof_stages.summary: The proof first builds an infinite p-adic Sudoku puzzle using the last nonzero base-p digit function, line-slope constraints, and an added column constraint, obtaining solvable puzzles all of whose good solutions have no vertical period.

proof_stages.label: Tiling language for first-order constraints
proof_stages.summary: A tiling-encoding language is introduced in which graph, permutation, and related first-order constraints can be expressed as systems of tiling equations and then compressed into a single tiling equation by adding a cyclic factor.

proof_stages.label: Sudoku-to-aperiodic monotile completion
proof_stages.summary: The good Sudoku constraints are encoded as one tiling equation in a virtually Z^2 group, yielding an aperiodic translational tile, after which pullback, inflation, and folded-bridge connectification give high-dimensional and connected counterexamples.

proof_stages.label: Domino problem to decorated Sudoku
proof_stages.summary: For the undecidability result, the classical undecidable domino problem is recalled and encoded into a decorated version of the p-adic Sudoku puzzle by matching domino data with p-adic scale information.

proof_stages.label: Undecidability and consequences
proof_stages.summary: The decorated Sudoku encoding is translated into a translational monotiling problem, proving undecidability, and the talk closes by relating undecidability back to aperiodicity and listing open problems about dimension, continuous/discrete equivalence, strong aperiodicity, disks, and symbolic-dynamical applications.
